title: The System You Choose After Repair
pressure: The incident has been addressed publicly enough that people expect closure, but the staff are watching whether the repair becomes a new operating standard or a polished ending.
tradeoff: Preserve authority flexibility or accept a visible system that limits selective judgment.

incident.roleShift: from repair owner to system identity chooser

propagation.exitEffect: Repair becomes a completed story while the original authority pattern remains available.
propagation.entryEffect: Repair becomes the first proof that the organization will limit its own power under pressure.
propagation.reExposureNote: The next conflict will test whether this was a message, a mood, or a system.

action_contract.description: Choose the operating system that will govern future integrity conflicts and make one observable commitment that limits selective authority.

AIR_logic.success: The decision creates a visible constraint that applies upward, laterally, and downward.
AIR_logic.miss: The decision creates emotional closure without changing the authority pattern that produced the incident.

forced_reset.trigger: A staff member asks whether the new integrity standard applies when a partner doctor, regional manager, or close ally is involved.
forced_reset.action: Restate the standard, name how it applies to leadership, and commit to the first observable check.

pattern_detection.authority_preservation: Uses repair language while keeping final judgment private.
pattern_detection.relationship_exception: Applies standards differently when trust, loyalty, or history is involved.
pattern_detection.closure_rush: Treats reduced conflict as proof that the system has changed.
pattern_detection.principle_without_constraint: States values but avoids a mechanism that can challenge leadership.

A.escalation_text: After the closing message, a lead assistant says privately that the words were good, but no one knows what will happen when a favored doctor breaks the same standard.
A.pressure_increase: The issue shifts from communication quality to whether leadership can be tested.

action_decision.prompt: What will you do within 72 hours so this does not end as a message-only repair?
action_decision.context: The team has heard closure language, but they have not seen a mechanism that can challenge leadership.

choices.label: Within 72 hours, send a written leadership-review trigger defining when integrity concerns involving doctors, managers, or partners must be reviewed by more than one leader.
Tell staff that your door is open and that you will personally handle future concerns with seriousness.

second_choices.label: Tell the lead assistant that future issues can be brought directly to leadership
second_choices.cost: Keeps the pathway dependent on personal access and trust.

second_choices.label: Create a written leadership-review trigger for future integrity concerns
second_choices.cost: Makes leadership review less controllable and more visible.

B.escalation_text: A regional manager reports that staff believe the new standard is real for weaker employees but negotiable for people leadership depends on.
B.pressure_increase: The repair is now being interpreted as another form of favoritism.

action_decision.prompt: What will you do within 72 hours to show that closeness does not change the standard?
action_decision.context: The system will be judged by how it treats people with influence, not by how it treats easy cases.

choices.label: Within 72 hours, select one active leadership-related follow-up and document it using the same standard, timeline, and visibility required for non-leadership staff.
Speak individually with trusted leaders and ask them to model the standard going forward.

second_choices.label: Privately coach the trusted people so they do not feel publicly exposed
second_choices.cost: Preserves loyalty but reinforces the belief that closeness changes consequences.

second_choices.label: Apply the same documentation and follow-up standard to trusted people first
second_choices.cost: Risks disappointment from people who expected relational protection.

C.escalation_text: Two weeks later, a similar conflict appears in another office. The details are different, but the same pattern appears: unclear authority, private exceptions, and staff waiting to see who is protected.
C.pressure_increase: The incident is no longer local; it is becoming a transferable operating pattern.

action_decision.prompt: What will you do within 72 hours to prevent the pattern from traveling office to office?
action_decision.context: The pressure is now organizational. Local repair without system change will keep reproducing the same conflict.

choices.label: Within 72 hours, create a cross-office integrity check with three required questions: who has authority, who receives exceptions, and who can challenge the decision.
Ask the regional manager to monitor the new office closely and notify you if the situation escalates.

second_choices.label: Handle the new office separately because every location has different dynamics
second_choices.cost: Avoids overgeneralizing but allows the same pattern to restart under a new story.

second_choices.label: Convert the repeated pattern into a cross-office integrity check
second_choices.cost: Requires admitting that the first incident revealed a system issue, not just a local issue.

D.escalation_text: Once you define the leadership standard, a partner doctor asks whether this means non-partner staff can question partner decisions without going through the usual chain of command.
D.pressure_increase: The choice now threatens hierarchy, speed, and partner comfort.

action_decision.prompt: What will you do within 72 hours to make the integrity system real when hierarchy resists it?
action_decision.context: The system becomes real only when it can slow down authority in a defined, observable way.

choices.label: Within 72 hours, publish a narrow challenge pathway allowing staff to raise integrity concerns involving partners to a defined two-person review outside the direct chain of command.
Explain to partners that the standard is not meant to undermine authority and should be used with professional judgment.

second_choices.label: Clarify that the standard supports feedback but does not change authority structure
second_choices.cost: Protects hierarchy but weakens the first real test of the system.

second_choices.label: Define a narrow challenge pathway that applies even when a partner is involved
second_choices.cost: Creates discomfort for partners and requires you to accept review without controlling the first interpretation.

ending_identity_choice.prompt: After this incident, which system are you choosing to become?

choices.label: A system where repair means conflict is lowered and leadership keeps final discretion private
A system where repair creates a visible constraint that can question leadership under pressure
Close the incident with a careful message about lessons learned
choices.subtext: It feels responsible because people are tired and another heavy conversation may reopen tension.

choices.label: Protect the relationships that helped stabilize the office
choices.subtext: It feels practical because losing trusted people could create another operational crisis.

choices.label: Move the team forward and focus on the next urgent office issue
choices.subtext: It feels efficient because the organization has too many active fires to stay in one incident.

choices.label: Define the integrity system that will apply when leadership is the pressure source
choices.subtext: It costs authority comfort because the standard will be used to question your future decisions.
